content.full_text:
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of two board members of the
Grec-nstone Community Services.District regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
P<-rtitical Reform Act (the "Act")l and is based on the facts presented; the niir political Practices
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re
Oglesby (L975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, we base our advice solely on the provisions of
the Act and do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as
common law conflict of interest, Government Code Section 1090, or the incompatible offices
provisions of Govemment Code Section1099. B-ecause you seek general information, we are
treating this as a request for informal assistance.z
Fern PolrrrcAr- PnncrrcES CoMMrssroN
-128 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA 958 14-2-329
(916) 322--s660 0 Fax (916) 322-0886
April 8,2010
George Amo
General Manager
Greenstone Country Community Services District
3451 Stagecoach Road
Placerville, California 95667
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.I-10-042
Dear Mr. Amo:
QUESTTON
Can a boardmember who sits on both the Greenstone homeowner's association board, a
nonprofit association, and the Greenstone Country Community Services District participate and
vote in governmental decisions to allocate funds to the homeowners' board?
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections I 8 I l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
I Infbrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provicled by an opinion or formal
rvritten advice. (Section 831 l4; regulation 18329(cX3), copy enclosed).


File No. I-10-042
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
Yes. A boardmember may participate and vote in governmental decisions to allocate
funds to the homeowners' board on behalf of the community services district so long as the
decision does not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of the
boardmembers' economic interest as discussed below.
FACTS
You are the General Manager of the Greenstone Country Community Services District
(the "GCCSD"), a Local Area Formation Commission (LAFCO). Two of the GCCSD board
members also sit as board members for the Greenstone Country Homeowners Association
("GCHA"), a nonprofit organization. Members of the GCCSD are elected by the residents of
Greenstone Countryi (the "Community") and the members must reside in the Community to
serve on the GCCSD board. The jurisdiction of the GCCSD does not extend beyond the
Community.
A percentage of the property taxes collected within the Community go to fund the
GCCSD, along with a special assessment of $100 per year per. improved parcel. By its charter
GCCSD funds, through the homeowners association, the upkeep of riding and walking trails,
parks, lakes, dams, and further provides funds for security within the Community. For example,
the GCCSD is currently considering an allocation to the homeowners association of $122,000 to
upgrade its walking and riding trails. These trails run throughout the Community and service all
of the properties located within.
You have indicated that money provided to the homeowners association for these projects
will have an effect on property values within the Community.
PorsNrIll, CoNFLrcr oF INTEREST
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
The Commission has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a govemmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her
financial interests.
I Greenstone Country is a private gated community near Placerville, California consisting of over 2,200
acres with 347 parcels of 3.5 acres or more, 323 of which are improved, with riding and walking trails, parks, lakes,
and other amenities.


File No. l-10-042
Page No. 3
SrBps I &2: Anr GCCSD Bolnn*rElrBERs Punrrc Orrrcnr,s MAKTNG, PanrrcrperrNc rN
MlxlNc, oR INFLUENCING A GovERNMENTAL Dnctston?
A GCCSD boardmember is a public official under the Act. (Section 82048.)
Consequently, members may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use their official
position to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on any of their economic interests. Because boardmembers will be called upon to consider
proposed decisions before the GCCSD, they will be making, participating in making, or otherwise
using their official position to influence a govemmental decision.
Srrp 3: Dors rHE BoARDMEMBERS Hevr l PorrNrrALLy DrsqualmyrNc EcoNoMrc
INrpnrsr?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any one of five enurnerated economic interests, including:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)
a
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(c); Regulation 1 8703.3.)
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within l2 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family - this is the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5.)
You have indicated that each boardmember owns his or her own home within the
Community. You have not provided any information with respect to any other economic
interest. Accordingly, our analysis is Iimited to each boardmember's economic interest in his or
her home.
a
o


File No. l-10-O42
Page No.4
SrBp 4: Is Tun Ecoxopttc lxrpnrsr Drnrcrly oR INDTRECTLy lNvor,vnn rN THE
GovrnNuENTAL DpcrsroN?a
"[n order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the official's
economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the governmental decision."
(Regulation 1870a(a).) For governmental decisions that affect real property interests, the
standards set forth in Regulation 18704.2 apply. (Regulation 18704(a)(2).) For govemmental
decisions that affect business entities, the standards set forth in Regulation IS7O4.t apply.
(Regulation I 870a(a)( I ).)
Real Property:
Regulation 18704.2(a) states, in pertinent part:
"(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is
directly involved in a governmental decision if any of the following apply:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property [that] is the subject of the governmental decision."
However, Regulation 18704.2(b)(2) provides that notwithstanding the above rule, real
property is not directly involved if the govemmental decision solely concerns repairs,
replacement, or maintenance of existing streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities
Therefore, if the decision is to allocate funds for the improvement of the walking and
riding trails, parks, Iakes, dams, etc. and the trails, park, lake, dams, etc. are within 500 feet of
the official's real property, the official's economic interest is directly involved. On the other
hand, if the trails, park, lake, damns, etc. that are the subject of the decision are not within 500
feet of the official's real property, or the decision is for ftrnding the maintenance of those areas
and not for improvements, the official's economic interest is indirectly involved in the
govemmental decision.
Other than the decision regarding the allocation of funds for upgrading the walking and
riding trails, you have not provided any specific information regarding a govemmental decision.
Srrp 5: Mlrpnrllrry STANDARD
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
govemmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a).)
a If a public offlcial's economic interest in not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is considered
"indirectly involved." (Regulation I 8704(a).)


File No. l-10-042
Page No. 5
Directly Involved Property: For directly involved real property, Regulation
18705.2(a)(l) provides that the financial effect of a govemmental decision on real property is
presumed to be material.
"The presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the govemmental decision will have any financial effect on the
real property." (Regulation 18705.2(a)(l). (Emphasis added.)
Under this rule, the financial effect of the decision is material even if it has only a one
penny effect. This is known as the "one-penny ruIe." [n order to rebut the presumption, the
official must establish that the decision would not affect his or her property;s value by even one
cent.
Indirectly Involved Property: For indirectly involved real property, Regulation
18705.2(b)(l) provides that the financial effect of a govemmental decision on ieal property,
other than leaseholds, is presumed not to be material.
"This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific
circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the
nature of the real property if which the public official has an economic interest,
which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a mateiial
financial effect on the real property in which the public official has an interest.
Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered include, but are not
limited to, circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.,,
In summary, if the property that is the subject of the governmental decision is within 500 feet
of the official's home, he or she will have a presumed conflict of interest unless he or she can show
that the financial effect will not be even one-penny. If the property that is the subject of the
governmental decision is not within 500 feet of the official's home or involves miintenance, rather
than improvement to the walking or riding trails, parks, Iakes, dams, etc. the official will not have
a conflict of interest unless the governmental decision is of such a magnitude that it affects properties
more than 500 feet away, including the official's property.


File No. l-10-O42
Page No. 6
Srrp 6: RBaSoxABLY Fonnsm.ure
Whether the financial consequences of a governmental decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time the decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A
financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more
than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Ultimately, it is up to the public official to make the determination through a good faith
effort. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-113; Moock Advice Letter, No. A-01-140; O'Harra
Advice Letter, No. A-00-174.)
SrBp 7: Punr,rc GnNen.lr,ly
The public generally exception provides that "notwithstanding a determination that a
governmental decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on a public
official's economic interest, the public official will not have a disqualifying conflict of interest if
the financial effect on the official's economic interest is indistinguishable from the manner in
which the decision will affect the public generally. (Regulation 18707(a).)
A financial effect on a public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its
effect on the public generally if the governmental decision affects a significant segment of the
public in substantially the same manner as it affects the official. (Regulation 18707.1(a).) For
real property economic interests, a significant segment is defined as ten percent or more of all
property owners or residential property owners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency.
You have stated that the walking and riding trails run within 500 feet of "most of the
properties" in the Community, which is the jurisdiction that the officials represent. So long and
the governmental decision you mentioned is to improve all of the trails (or at least those portions
running within 500 feet of ten percent of the properties), the significant segment test will be met.
Additionally, so long as any improvement to the portions of the trails running within 500 feet of
the official's property are not significantly dissimilar to the other improvements to the trails, the
govemmental decision will affect the significant segment in substantially the same manner as it
affects the official's property, and the public generally exception will apply.
SrBp Ercnt: LBcar,r,y REeUTRED PARTICTrATToN
You have not given any facts indicating that either the "public generally" exception or
that the "legally required participation" exception would apply.


File No. l-10-042
Page No. 7
If you have any questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
By: Wil
Lenkeit
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
WJL:jgl
Enclosure

boardmembers' economic interest as discussed below.
